title: Probabilistic Data Structures — Bloom Filters, HyperLogLog, Count-Min Sketch & MinHash
description: Probabilistic data structures trade perfect accuracy for massive memory savings — a critical tool in every Staff+ engineer's system design toolkit. Covers Bloom filters (set membership), HyperLogLog (cardinality estimation), Count-Min Sketch (frequency estimation), and MinHash (set similarity). Includes use cases from cache deduplication to real-time analytics at FAANG scale and how SAP's ABAP internal table hash algorithms provide a basic conceptual bridge.

description.value:
The problem: You need to check whether an item is in a set — but the set is too large to store in memory. Example: a web crawler has visited 10 billion URLs. Checking each new URL against the visited set would require a database query (slow) or holding all URLs in memory (impossible — 10 billion URLs at ~100 bytes each = 1 TB).

The data structure: A Bloom filter is a bit array of size m with k independent hash functions. Operations: (1) Add (item): hash the item with each of k hash functions to get k positions in the bit array. Set all k positions to 1. (2) Query (item): hash the item with each of k hash functions. If any of the k positions is 0, the item is definitely NOT in the set. If all k positions are 1, the item is probably in the set (but could be a false positive).

Parameters and tradeoffs: (1) m (bits) — larger m = lower false positive rate. (2) k (hash functions) — optimal k = (m/n) * ln(2), where n is the expected number of items. (3) False positive rate ≈ (1 - e^(-kn/m))^k. Example: for n = 10 million items, m = 80 million bits (10 MB), k = 6 hash functions → false positive rate ≈ 2%. The cost: we can store a set of 10 million items in 10 MB with a 2% false positive rate — vs 1 GB+ for exact storage. (4) Bloom filters cannot delete items (standard version). Counting Bloom filters extend this by using counters instead of bits, allowing deletion.

FAANG use cases: (1) Web crawler deduplication (Google) — check if a URL has been crawled. (2) Cache deduplication (Facebook) — check if an item is in the cache before making an expensive query. (3) Malicious URL detection (Chrome) — check a URL against a Bloom filter of known malicious URLs. (4) Cassandra's LSM tree — Bloom filters on each SSTable to avoid disk reads for keys that don't exist. (5) Bitcoin SPV (Simplified Payment Verification) — lightweight wallets check transaction inclusion using Bloom filters.

SAP Bridge: ABAP's internal table hash lookup uses a hash table with collision chaining — an exact set membership structure. A Bloom filter is the 'approximate' version of a hash table — it uses much less memory but trades the ability to say 'definitely yes' for 'maybe yes.' The concept of false positives (a hash collision in ABAP's hash table) is familiar — a Bloom filter amplifies that tradeoff intentionally to save memory.

The problem: Count the number of unique elements in a multiset (e.g., unique visitors to a website, unique search queries, unique IP addresses) — without storing all unique elements. For a site with 100 million daily visitors, storing each visitor ID would require gigabytes.

The insight: If you have a hash function that produces uniformly distributed bits, the number of leading zeros in the binary hash value follows a pattern: on average, 1 in 2^k hash values has exactly k leading zeros. If you observe a hash value with k leading zeros, the cardinality (number of unique elements) is approximately 2^k. This is the 'bite the bullet' observation — HyperLogLog optimizes it.

Algorithm: (1) Hash each element to a uniformly distributed 64-bit value. (2) Use the first p bits to select one of 2^p registers. (3) In the selected register, store the maximum number of leading zeros observed in the remaining bits of the hash. (4) The cardinality estimate is computed from the harmonic mean of all register values: E = α_m * m^2 * (Σ 2^(-M[j]))^(-1), where m = 2^p, M[j] is the value in register j, and α_m is a bias correction constant.

Accuracy and memory: (1) Standard error: ~1.04 / sqrt(m). With m = 1024 registers (about 6KB memory), standard error ~3%. With m = 16384 (about 12KB), standard error ~0.8%. (2) To estimate cardinality up to 2^64 (~10^19), you need only ~12KB of memory — vs GBs for exact counting. (3) HyperLogLog supports union: the union of two HyperLogLogs is computed by taking the max of each register (no loss of precision). This enables distributed cardinality estimation: each node maintains its own HLL, and the central coordinator merges them.

FAANG use cases: (1) Redshift/BigQuery approximate count distinct. (2) Google Analytics unique visitor counting — each web server maintains a local HLL, merged at query time. (3) Netflix — unique content views per region. (4) Cloudflare — unique IP addresses per customer across thousands of edge servers.

The problem: Estimate the frequency of any element in a stream — how many times has this search query appeared? How many requests has this IP address made? Storing exact counts for millions of distinct elements is memory-prohibitive.

The data structure: A Count-Min Sketch (CMS) is a 2D array of counters: d rows (depth) and w columns (width). It has d hash functions (one per row). Operations: (1) Add (item, count=1): for each row i, compute h_i(item) → column index j, increment counter[i][j] by count. (2) Query (item): for each row i, compute h_i(item) → column index j, read counter[i][j]. Return the minimum counter value across all d rows (the 'min' in Count-Min Sketch).

Accuracy and tradeoffs: (1) The CMS always OVERestimates frequency — it never underestimates (the true count is ≤ the estimated count). The overestimate comes from hash collisions (other items incrementing the same counter). (2) The estimate is within ε*n of the true count with probability 1-δ, where ε = e/w (w = columns) and δ = e^(-d) (d = rows). Example: w = 2000, d = 5 → error bound ~0.1% of total count (ε ≈ e/2000 ≈ 0.00136) with probability 99.3%. Memory: w*d*sizeof(counter) = 2000*5*4 = 40KB. (3) Count-Mean Sketch (improved variant) subtracts the estimated average noise from each counter, reducing bias.

FAANG use cases: (1) Heavy hitters — find the most frequent search queries in a streaming fashion (Google). (2) Network monitoring — identify top talkers (IP addresses with most traffic) on a router (Cloudflare). (3) Rate limiting — estimate request count per user without storing per-user counters (you query the sketch instead of a per-key counter). (4) SQL query frequency estimation — which queries are run most often (Snowflake).

The problem: Given two sets, how similar are they? The Jaccard similarity = |A ∩ B| / |A ∪ B|. For sets with millions of elements (e.g., 'all documents in the Google index'), computing the exact intersection/union is too expensive.

The MinHash algorithm: (1) Represent each set by a fixed-size signature (e.g., 128 hashes). (2) To compute the signature: apply k independent hash functions to each element of the set; for each hash function, keep the minimum hash value observed. (3) The signature is the vector of k minimum hash values. (4) To estimate Jaccard similarity between two sets, compare their signatures: similarity ≈ (number of positions where signatures match) / k.

Why it works: The probability that two sets have the same minimum hash value (from the same hash function) is exactly |A ∩ B| / |A ∪ B| — the Jaccard similarity. This is the core insight: the MinHash signature preserves similarity in expectation. With k hash functions, the estimate converges with standard error ≈ 1/sqrt(k). For k = 256, standard error ≈ 6%.

Weighted MinHash (Google's variant): Google uses a variant called Weighted MinHash for document deduplication in search results. Each document is split into shingles (n-grams) — contiguous sequences of words. The set of shingles represents the document. Two documents with similar sets of shingles are near-duplicates. Google's search index uses MinHash to: (1) Detect near-duplicate pages and cluster them (showing only one representative in search results). (2) Cluster news articles by topic (different sources writing about the same event). (3) Identify plagiarized content.

SAP Bridge: ABAP's standard similarity algorithms (the function module SCTC_GET_STRING_SIMILARITY, or the old SPFLI-style Levenshtein distance) compute exact string similarity. MinHash is the approximate, scalable version of the same concept — instead of comparing every pair of documents, you compare their fixed-size signatures. Any SAP engineer who has worked with master data deduplication (merging duplicate customer/vendor records) understands the problem — MinHash is how FAANG solves it at 100M+ records.

details: FAANG Interview Tip: Probabilistic data structures are asked in Staff+ system design interviews at Google, Meta, and Netflix, and in dedicated 'algorithms and data structures' rounds for infrastructure roles. The key signals: (1) Knowing which structure to use for which problem — Bloom (set membership), HLL (count distinct), CMS (frequency), MinHash (similarity). (2) Understanding the memory-accuracy tradeoff parameters — not just what they do, but how to tune them. (3) Real FAANG use cases — shows you've used them in production. SAP engineers should frame this as: 'I understand exact set operations from ABAP internal tables; now I need approximate versions for internet-scale problems.'
